title: Chapter 18: The First Seat
content: The Upper Chamber was not a dimension this time. It was a room. A real room, with walls and a ceiling and a floor that didn't float or shift. Wood paneling. A long table. Seven chairs, three of which were occupied.

Roxanne sat in the Second Seat's position. Across from her, the Third Seat — the old man — sat in his usual place, gray suit, peaceful expression. And at the head of the table, in the First Seat's position, sat a woman.

She was tall. Impossibly tall — even seated, she towered over the other chairs. Her skin was pale, almost translucent, and her eyes were solid black, no iris, no white. She wore a dress that seemed to be woven from the same violet-black material as the apocalypse sky.
She was the First Seat. The oldest living power in the Nether-Grid's governing body. The one who had been silent for three centuries.
She was no longer silent.
"You're young," the First Seat said. Her voice was not loud. It did not need to be. The room listened to her.
"I've been told."
"The Third Seat put you here. I want to know why."
"Because the old powers are fading," Roxanne said. "Because the balance is shifting. Because he needed someone who wasn't part of the old system."
"And you think you're not part of the old system?"
"I've been in the Nether-Grid for exactly eight days. I don't think I'm part of anything."
The First Seat's black eyes studied her.
"Do you know what the Primordial card really is?"
"It's my birthright."

"It's a key. The Primordial Void — the original entity from which all of existence emerged — is not dead. It's dormant. The card is a key to its core. Whoever holds the card can, in theory, access the Void's heart."
She leaned forward.
"The Third Seat didn't put you in power because you're clever. He put you in power because you're the only person alive who can open the Void."
Roxanne's blood went cold.
She turned to the old man.
"Is this true?"
The Third Seat adjusted his cuff. "It's one interpretation."
"One interpretation?"
"The Primordial Void is not a lock. It's a door. And doors can be opened from either side."
He looked at her. His gray eyes were unreadable.
"The Void has been trying to open itself for twelve thousand years. Your bloodline was the seal that kept it closed. Now the seal is awake — and walking around with a credit card."
"I'm the seal?"
"You're the last surviving lock. The card is the key. The same object. You are both the thing that keeps the door closed and the thing that opens it."
Roxanne's hand went to the card in her pocket.
"So if I use the card — if I really use it — I might open the Void?"
"You might. Or you might strengthen the seal. No one has ever been in your position before. The bloodline was supposed to train for this. You inherited it by accident."
"Wonderful," she said flatly.
"Indeed."
Silas took her aside after the summoning stone faded.
"He's playing you."
"I know."
"If he put you in the Second Seat, he has a plan for what he wants you to do there."
"I know."
"And he's not going to tell you what it is until you're in too deep to back out."
"I know."
She sat down on the edge of the dean's desk. Looked at the card in her hands.

"But here's the thing: he put me in a position where I can actually do what I wanted to do anyway. Free people. Break contracts. Change the system. If his plan happens to align with that, I don't care if I'm being played."
"And if his plan diverges?"
"Then I'll buy my way out. I have an infinite card. I can afford to change my mind."
Silas regarded her with those ancient red eyes.
"You're either the most pragmatic person I've ever met, or the most naive."
"Can I be both?"
"You already are."
The Exchange had a rhythm. Roxanne was learning to feel it.
Every transaction created a ripple. Every contract signed shifted the balance somewhere. The whole system was an enormous, interconnected web of debt and credit, and she was now a node at its center.
"We need to move faster," she said, walking through the campus. "The Council will regroup. They'll find a loophole. We need to secure more territory, more resources, more leverage before they do."
"You can't buy everything at once," Silas said.
"Watch me."
She held up the card.
*DISPLAY: ALL AVAILABLE DEBT CONTRACTS — EASTERN SECTOR.*
The screen filled with thousands of entries. Personal debts. Business debts. Soul contracts. Each with a buyout price, each representing a person or entity bound to the old system.
*TOTAL: 47,293 CONTRACTS. TOTAL BUYOUT: 12,400,000 OBOLS.*
"Purchase all."
*APPROVED. 12,400,000 OBOLS DEBITED. 47,293 CONTRACTS FORGIVEN.*
The Exchange feed updated. A wave of green icons spread across the map — debts cleared, obligations dissolved.
All across the city, people felt it. A weight lifting. A chain breaking. A sudden, inexplicable freedom.
Roxanne pocketed the card.
"That's a start."
"I want to see the original Charter," Roxanne said.
The Third Seat raised an eyebrow. "That document is not publicly accessible."
"I'm the Second Seat now. Make it accessible."
He studied her for a long moment. Then he nodded.
"Very well."
A portal opened in the floor of his dimension — a shaft of light descending into a space that felt older than anything Roxanne had encountered. Older than the Hollow Cathedral. Older than Silas.
She stepped through.

The Archive was a library that existed outside of space. Books floated in crystalline spheres, each one containing a complete legal code from a different era of the Nether-Grid's existence. The earliest ones were inscribed on stone tablets, their text glowing faintly.
And at the center of it all, encased in a pillar of solidified time, was the original Primordial Charter.
It was a single sheet of parchment, yellowed with age, written in a script that predated human writing. But Roxanne could read it. The card translated automatically.

*"We, the undersigned Primordials, do hereby establish the Nether-Grid Exchange as a neutral ground for the transaction of all things. The Exchange shall be governed by three seats, each held by a different Primordial lineage. The First Seat shall hold authority over all matters. The Second Seat shall adjudicate disputes. The Third Seat shall maintain the records."*
"The Second Seat adjudicates disputes," she read aloud. "Not enforces contracts. Not collects debts. Adjudicates."
"Correct."
"So the Arbiter was never supposed to be a debt collector. She was a judge."
"She drifted from her original purpose over the millennia."
"How much did she drift?"
"Significantly."
Roxanne looked at the Charter again.
"Time for a course correction."
